sourcePage.rawText:
19   A polygon has n vertices, where n  3. It has the following properties:

         •   Every vertex of the polygon lies on the circumference of a circle C.
         •   The centre of the circle C is inside the polygon.
         •   The radii from the centre of the circle C to the vertices of the polygon cut the polygon
             into n triangles of equal area.

     For which values of n are these properties sufficient to deduce that the polygon is regular?

     A   no values of n

     B   n ൌ 3 only

     C   n ൌ 3 and n ൌ 4 only

     D   n ൌ 3 and n  5 only

     E   all values of n




                                                  21

stem.rawText:
A polygon has n vertices, where n  3. It has the following properties:

         •   Every vertex of the polygon lies on the circumference of a circle C.
         •   The centre of the circle C is inside the polygon.
         •   The radii from the centre of the circle C to the vertices of the polygon cut the polygon
             into n triangles of equal area.

     For which values of n are these properties sufficient to deduce that the polygon is regular?

solution.rawText:
We sketch a part of the polygon with the triangles described in the question:




                                                  θ

Suppose the angle of one of the triangles is θ, as shown. Then the area of the triangle is given
by 12 r2 sin θ where r is the radius of the circle (using the formula A = 12 ab sin C).
Since r is the same for each of the triangles, all the triangles will have equal area if and only if
sin θ is the same for each triangle. This will certainly be the case if the polygon is regular, but
we have to determine if this can be true in any other situation.
If two of the triangles have angles at the centre of the circle of θ and φ, then sin φ = sin θ if and
only if φ = θ or φ = π − θ. Can we have the latter possibility? If so, it would mean we have
some angles being θ and some being π − θ.
Suppose then that θ is acute and π − θ is obtuse. (We cannot have a reflex angle as the centre
of the circle lies inside the polygon, and if θ is a right angle, then π − θ = θ.) Suppose further
that k of the angles of the polygon triangles equal π − θ and the remaining n − k equal θ. Then
the sum of these triangle angles is given by

                                     (n − k)θ + k(π − θ) = 2π

since they form a whole circle. We can rearrange this to (n − 2k)θ = (2 − k)π and hence

                                                (2 − k)π
                                           θ=            .
                                                 n − 2k

There can be at most three obtuse angled triangles (as four would be more than a whole circle),
so let’s try k = 1, k = 2 and k = 3 in turn.
                          π
If k = 1, we have θ = n−2   . It looks as though this will work for every n, but we have to be
a little careful as we need θ to be acute. When n = 3, we get θ = π, which is not allowed.
When n = 4, we get θ = π2 , which is again not allowed. When n > 4, θ < π2 , so this does work.
Therefore when n ≥ 5, it is possible for the conditions to be satisfied but for the polygon not to
be regular.
                                   0
Now consider k = 2, giving θ = n−4    . We only need to consider n = 3 and n = 4, as we have
dealt with n ≥ 5 above. This is zero when n = 3, so it does not work for a triangle. When n = 4,
this formula is not meaningful (it gives 00 ). We can instead go back to the original equation
(n − 2k)θ = (2 − k)π, which becomes 0 = 0 in this case, meaning that it works for any value
of θ. And indeed, this gives two angles of θ and two of π − θ, so we get a non-regular polygon
with equal area triangles. The simplest example is a non-square rectangle.
We are left with the case k = 3 to consider. When n = 3, there would be no acute angles
and three obtuse ones, and the formula gives θ = −π   π
                                                 −3 = 3 . And indeed, a triangle with three

Test of Mathematics for University Admission, 2022 Paper 2 Solutions


obtuse angles of π3 is an equilateral triangle satisfying the conditions. Therefore the only triangle
(n = 3) satisfying the conditions is regular.
We have thus been able to construct non-regular polygons satisfying the conditions for every
n ≥ 4, but not for n = 3. Hence the correct answer is option B.
